title: Piping smooth sides
subtitle: Flat, clean buttercream sides on a layer cake
excerpt: The technique for achieving straight, smooth buttercream sides on a stacked layer cake using a turntable, bench scraper, and two coats. Crumb coat first to seal, chill, then final coat and scrape level.
sourceNotes: The crumb coat and final coat method for smooth cake sides is standard in professional patisserie. The technique using a turntable and bench scraper is documented in cake-decorating textbooks from the mid-twentieth century and is the basis for all modern smooth-finish cake decoration.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: A crumb-coated cake can be refrigerated overnight before applying the final coat.

glossaryTerms.definition: A thin initial layer of buttercream applied to a layer cake to seal in crumbs before the final coat. The crumb coat is chilled until firm, trapping any loose cake fragments so they don't contaminate the smooth outer layer. Standard practice for all professionally finished buttercream cakes.

content.text: Smooth buttercream sides come from two coats, not one. The first is the

: a thin seal that traps crumbs and provides a stable surface. The second is the final coat, applied after chilling and scraped level with a bench scraper and turntable. Rushing the chill between coats is the single most common reason for a streaky result.

The bench scraper must be held at 90° to the cake, not angled inward. Angling inward creates a tapered top edge rather than a flat side. Place the scraper against the cake and rotate the turntable toward you while keeping the scraper still.

Place the bottom cake layer on a cake board slightly smaller than the cake. Put a small blob of buttercream on the turntable to anchor the board. Spread buttercream between each layer and stack the cake. Apply a thin, uneven layer of buttercream over the entire outside of the cake using the offset spatula, working it into any gaps. This is the crumb coat: it does not need to look good. Use the bench scraper to roughly level the sides, holding it vertically and rotating the turntable. Refrigerate for at least 30 minutes until the crumb coat is firm.

Apply a generous final coat of buttercream to the sides and top using the offset spatula. Work in sections, building up the buttercream slightly thicker than you want the final depth to be. Smooth the top roughly with the spatula.

Hold the bench scraper flat against the side of the cake, touching the board at the base, at a strict 90° to the cake surface. Rotate the turntable steadily, letting the scraper remove excess buttercream. Dip the scraper in warm water and wipe clean between passes. For a sharp top edge, use the offset spatula to bring the slight overhang of buttercream on the sides inward over the top edge, working in toward the centre. Refrigerate for 15 minutes before adding any further decoration.
